name: chicken with vinegar
instructions:
TWO VARIANTS on the french chicken + wine + vinegar shape. Variant A is Samin Nosrat's canonical version (chicken + wine + vinegar + over-caramelized onions + cream finish). Variant B is a Nosrat-plus-Prune-inspired take (Alexandra's Kitchen, drawing on both Samin's Salt Fat Acid Heat and Gabrielle Hamilton's Prune) — adds tomato paste + garlic + hot sauce, skips the cream, uses chicken thighs specifically.

=== VARIANT A — SAMIN NOSRAT'S POULET AU VINAIGRE ===

From Salt Fat Acid Heat (pp. 336-337). A superior version of chicken fricassee — the fricassee recipe used to live in this cookbook but was removed after too many disappointing attempts. This variant replaces it in spirit and gets the register right.

INGREDIENTS (Nosrat's recipe, verbatim):
- 1 whole 4-pound chicken (broken down into 8 pieces — see notes)
- salt (generous; pre-salt the night before if possible)
- freshly ground black pepper
- 1/2 cup all-purpose flour
- extra-virgin olive oil (enough to coat the pan for browning)
- 3 tbsp unsalted butter
- 2 medium yellow onions, thinly sliced
- 3/4 cup dry white wine
- 6 tbsp white wine vinegar
- 2 tbsp fresh tarragon leaves, finely chopped (divided)
- 1/2 cup heavy whipping cream OR crème fraîche

PROCEDURE:
1. PRE-SALT THE CHICKEN: cut whole chicken into 8 pieces. Season generously with salt and pepper. Ideally night before (refrigerate); minimum 1 hr on counter.
2. DREDGE in salted flour, shake off excess.
3. BROWN in olive oil in 2 batches skin-side down (~4 min per side). Discard fat, wipe pan.
4. OVER-CARAMELIZE ONIONS 25 min in butter — deeply browned, almost burned.
5. DEGLAZE with wine + vinegar, add half the tarragon.
6. BRAISE: chicken back skin-side up, lid ajar. Pull breasts at ~12 min; dark meat 35-40 min total.
7. Splash water if pan dries out (see notes on tight liquid budget).
8. FINISH SAUCE: chicken to platter, add cream/crème fraîche, simmer to thicken. Taste and adjust with salt, pepper, MORE VINEGAR. Add remaining tarragon. Serves 4-6.

=== VARIANT B — ALEXANDRA'S KITCHEN / PRUNE-INSPIRED (WITH TOMATO PASTE + HOT SAUCE, NO CREAM) ===

Inspired by both Samin Nosrat's Salt Fat Acid Heat and Gabrielle Hamilton's Prune, via Alexandra's Kitchen. Uses chicken thighs specifically (not whole chicken), sweet onions, adds garlic + tomato paste + hot sauce, skips the cream finish. My modifications: bay leaf + herbes de provence instead of tarragon; Cholula instead of Tabasco. Serve with a simple kale salad on the side.

INGREDIENTS:
- 1/2 cup all-purpose flour
- kosher salt and freshly ground black pepper
- 1 tbsp olive oil
- 3 lb bone-in, skin-on chicken thighs
- 4 tbsp butter
- 2 medium SWEET onions, sliced thinly
- 1 clove garlic, minced
- 1 tbsp tomato paste
- 1 tsp hot sauce (Cholula preferred — see notes on why not Tabasco)
- 3/4 cup dry white wine
- 6 tbsp white wine vinegar
- ~1/2 cup chicken stock (my addition — see notes on why variant B uses stock where variant A doesn't)
- 1 bay leaf and a generous pinch of herbes de provence (my modification — replaces Alexandra's 2 tbsp thyme/tarragon)
- (optional) fresh thyme leaves at the finish for garnish

PROCEDURE:
1. Season flour with salt and pepper on a plate.
2. Add olive oil to a large Dutch oven over medium-high heat. When very hot, dredge chicken pieces in seasoned flour and add to pot in batches (3-4 pieces at a time — give them room to brown).
3. Let chicken sit UNDISTURBED at least 6 minutes to get a good sear. Flip and cook another 2-3 min. Remove to a paper-towel-lined plate. Don't need to be cooked through. Repeat with remaining chicken.
4. Discard fat carefully; wipe down pan. Turn heat to medium.
5. Add butter and sweet onions with salt and pepper. Cook until wilted and starting to caramelize, about 15-20 min (shorter than variant A's 25-min over-caramelization — see notes).
6. Add garlic; cook 2 min.
7. Turn heat up slightly. Stir in tomato paste and hot sauce; cook until the tomato paste darkens and looks toasty.
8. Add wine, vinegar, and chicken stock, scraping up the fond with a wooden spoon. Bring to a simmer.
9. Add bay leaf and herbes de provence. Return chicken to pot WITHOUT SUBMERGING (nestle in, skin above the liquid).
10. Cover with lid slightly ajar; simmer 25 min.
11. Remove chicken to a platter. Spoon sauce and onions on top. Fish out bay leaf. Optional fresh thyme garnish.
notes:
SUPERIOR VERSION OF CHICKEN FRICASSEE: this recipe replaces a chicken fricassee recipe that used to live in the cookbook and was removed after too many disappointing attempts. Both variants get the register right where the standard fricassee formula never quite did.

=== SHARED CORE — WHY BOTH VARIANTS WORK ===

OVER-CARAMELIZED ONIONS ARE THE FOUNDATIONAL TECHNIQUE (VARIANT A HARDER, VARIANT B SOFTER): both variants push the onion cook past the standard softened-golden stage; variant A goes further into edge-of-burnt territory (25 min), variant B stops at wilted-and-starting-to-caramelize (15-20 min). This is where the dish's structural umami comes from. Standard fricassee recipes stop earlier and rely on stock and mushrooms to fill the umami slot; here, the onions ARE the umami base.

VINEGAR — LOAD-BEARING ACID, NOT AN AFTERTHOUGHT: white wine vinegar is a full 6 tablespoons in both variants, not a splash. It cooks off the raw sharpness during the braise but the acid remains structurally present in the sauce. The recipe title ("chicken with VINEGAR") is honest — the vinegar is a headline ingredient, not a background note.

NO MUSHROOMS — 'UMAMI OVERLOAD' IS THE FRICASSEE PROBLEM: this is the specific insight that differentiates both variants from chicken fricassee. Fricassee recipes typically load mushrooms on top of the chicken-and-cream base, and the result reads muddy and umami-overloaded. Both variants leave mushrooms out entirely. If you're tempted to add mushrooms to either variant, resist.

STOCK IS VARIANT-SPECIFIC — OUT IN A, IN B: Nosrat's variant A explicitly avoids stock (part of the anti-umami-overload rule). Variant B uses it. ~1/2 cup is right — enough to help the braise, not so much that it turns the dish soupy. Bouillon cubes still clash in both variants (concentrated shortcut form; use real stock or nothing).

EXPLORATORY — WHY DOES VARIANT B NEED CHICKEN STOCK FOR UMAMI, NOT JUST LIQUID? this is the part I don't fully understand. Variant B has tomato paste (structural umami contributor) and I'd expect it to be MORE savory than variant A, which has no tomato. In practice it reads LESS savory without the stock. Two theories:

- THE 'CHEESY CREAM' THEORY: variant A adds cream to a very dry, very hot pan — by the time you're finishing the sauce, the wine and vinegar have mostly boiled away and the pan is concentrated fond + reduced-liquid + fat. When the cold cream hits that hot dry pan, it emulsifies into something that reads almost cheesy — a proto-cheese character that acts as a serious umami note. Variant B doesn't have this because there's no cream. Without the stock, you're left with tomato paste + water reduction, which is a sad sauce that lacks either the cream's cheesy depth OR a stock's meaty depth. Stock is the compensation. This theory says: cream in variant A is doing MORE than just enriching; it's a dry-pan-emulsification that generates cheesy umami, and variant B needs stock to replace that missing umami slot.

- THE 'ACIDITY OVERLOAD' THEORY: variant B has THREE acid sources — white wine, white wine vinegar, AND hot sauce (Cholula/Tabasco is vinegar-based). Variant A has only two (wine + vinegar; cream is neutral-to-slightly-tangy). The extra hot sauce might push variant B into acidity-overload territory where the sauce reads sour rather than savory, and chicken stock provides the sweetness/roundness needed to balance the extra acid. Onions and tomato paste can carry some of this balance but not all of it. This theory says: cream in variant A partly buffers the wine+vinegar acid via its fat and lactose; variant B's hot sauce adds a third acid source and needs stock to keep the acid-and-sweet in balance.

Both theories probably contribute something. The practical takeaway is the same either way: variant B genuinely needs the chicken stock, not as a liquid substitute but as a flavor structural element. Skipping it (using water) makes the dish read flat and sour in a way that variant A doesn't fall into.

=== VARIANT A NOTES ===

OVER-CARAMELIZED ONIONS — GO PAST STANDARD, THE FULL 25 MINUTES: variant A's onions should read genuinely dark, edge-of-burnt. Don't rush and don't stop early.

CREAM/CRÈME FRAÎCHE FINISH IS SMALL BUT LOAD-BEARING: only 1/2 cup, added at the end, simmered briefly to thicken. It's not a cream-based sauce; it's a wine-and-vinegar sauce that's tempered/enriched by cream. Variant B skips this entirely — see variant B note below on why.

BREAK DOWN A WHOLE CHICKEN — DON'T USE PRE-BUTCHERED PARTS: like all good french chicken recipes, this rewards buying a whole 4-pound chicken and breaking it down yourself into 8 pieces. Pre-butchered thighs/breasts UNDERWHELM — you lose the carcass (Nosrat says save for stock), skin-on integrity is often compromised, and you can't control cut sizes. Extra 10 min to butcher separates a great result from an okay one.

PRE-SALT — DO THE NIGHT BEFORE IF POSSIBLE: Nosrat's technique. Salt has time to diffuse rather than sit on the surface. Minimum 1 hr on the counter.

LIQUID BUDGET IS TIGHT — ADD WATER IF NEEDED: Nosrat's recipe has a tight liquid budget and can scorch before dark meat is done. Splash in water as needed to keep the braise going. She probably manages heat lower than I do; water is my safety valve.

LID AJAR IS DELIBERATE: concentrates the sauce during the cook. Sealing traps too much liquid; leaving off evaporates too fast.

PULL THE BREASTS EARLY: white meat done at ~12 min; dark meat wants 35-40. Two-stage removal keeps breast from drying out.

TASTE FOR VINEGAR AT THE FINISH: more vinegar (not more salt, not more cream) is the correction step if the sauce reads flat.

=== VARIANT B NOTES ===

CHICKEN THIGHS ONLY, NO WHOLE CHICKEN: variant B is written for 3 lb of bone-in, skin-on chicken thighs specifically. All-dark-meat means uniform cook time — no need to pull breasts early like variant A. Simpler timing, denser flavor.

SWEET ONIONS SPECIFICALLY: variant B calls for SWEET onions (Vidalia, Walla Walla, etc.), not yellow. Sweet onions caramelize faster and land at wilted-and-starting-to-caramelize at 15-20 min where yellows would still be softening. They also amplify the tomato-paste sweetness in the sauce.

SHORTER ONION COOK THAN VARIANT A — 15-20 MIN, NOT 25: variant B stops earlier because the tomato paste and hot sauce are contributing depth alongside the caramelization. Push past 20 min and you tip into variant-A over-caramelization territory, which pairs with cream better than with tomato paste.

TOMATO PASTE — SMALL AND TOASTED: 1 tbsp, cooked with the garlic until it darkens and 'looks toasty' (per Alexandra's phrasing). This is the umami-and-depth contribution that lets variant B skip the cream finish. Don't overdo — a full tomato-paste-forward sauce is a different dish.

HOT SAUCE — CHOLULA, NOT TABASCO (MY MODIFICATION): Alexandra's original calls for 1 tsp Tabasco. I tried it and Tabasco is a little too funky — its fermented character reads off against the sweet onions and tomato paste. Cholula is cleaner and more mild, and pairs better with the register variant B is going for. Same 1 tsp quantity.

BAY LEAF + HERBES DE PROVENCE INSTEAD OF TARRAGON (MY MODIFICATION): Alexandra's original calls for 2 tbsp thyme or tarragon. I prefer bay leaf + herbes de provence — the bay leaf sits quietly in the braise adding depth without competing, and herbes de provence carries a broader french provincial character that pairs cleanly with the tomato-paste + sweet-onion + hot-sauce trio. Tarragon and fresh thyme aren't wrong here (they'd match Alexandra's original intent), but the herbes de provence + bay combination is the version I've landed on.

NO CREAM FINISH IN VARIANT B: unlike variant A, variant B ends without cream/crème fraîche. The tomato paste + hot sauce + longer braise (25 min after wine goes in) build enough body that the cream isn't needed. Adding cream on top of variant B's ingredient list would tip the dish into a tomato-cream sauce that pulls away from the wine-and-vinegar identity.

CHICKEN NOT SUBMERGED — SKIN ABOVE THE LIQUID: variant B specifically nestles the chicken back into the pot WITHOUT submerging it — the skin should stay above the sauce line so it remains crisp rather than getting soggy in the braise. Alexandra's original is explicit about this.

SIMPLE KALE SALAD ON THE SIDE: Alexandra recommends serving with a simple kale salad. Cuts the richness of the braise.

=== SERVING ===

SERVE WITH STEAMED WHITE RICE (BOTH VARIANTS): rice soaks up the sauce cleanly. Mashed potatoes or buttered egg noodles also work. Crusty bread for mopping is fine as a supplement but rice/potatoes are the primary starch pairing.

=== RELATION TO OTHER RECIPES IN THE COOKBOOK ===

- CHICKEN FRICASSEE (REMOVED): this recipe explicitly supersedes chicken fricassee, which was removed for repeated disappointments.
- FRENCH CHICKEN SOUP: shares white wine + tarragon + chicken thighs family. Different format (broth + dumplings vs shallow-braise) but same french-chicken axis.
- COQ AU VIN: also a french chicken braise, but with red wine + mushrooms + lardons. Uses tomato-adjacent umami paths that chicken with vinegar deliberately avoids (see 'umami overload' note).
